creator: rometravelers
caption:
For much of the last century, the Pantheon was often presented as a triumph of Roman concrete, as though one miraculous building material somehow explained the survival of the monument. While Roman concrete is undoubtedly part of the story, the reality is considerably more sophisticated and, in my opinion, far more impressive.

The Pantheon survives because Roman engineers understood that a structure of this scale could not rely on any single innovation. The building stands on the former floodplain of the Tiber, a location that would hardly seem ideal for supporting the largest unreinforced concrete dome ever constructed. Rather than seeing this as an obstacle, Roman engineers designed an enormous foundation system capable of distributing the weight of the monument across a broad area.

The same logic can be seen throughout the building. The walls of the rotunda are exceptionally thick, the concrete becomes progressively lighter as it rises, the coffers reduce weight without compromising strength, and the great oculus eliminates the heaviest section of the dome altogether. Every design choice serves a structural purpose.

What continues to astonish me is that we often search for a single secret behind the Pantheon’s survival when the true achievement lies in the extraordinary combination of engineering solutions working together. Nearly two thousand years later, the monument remains standing not because the Romans discovered a miracle material, but because they understood how to solve a complex series of architectural and structural problems with remarkable elegance.

For those interested in exploring these subjects in greater depth, I regularly publish longer historical essays on Patreon and extended discussions on YouTube. If you are planning a visit to Rome and would like to experience these monuments with a historian and licensed guide, my contact information can be found on my profile.

What aspect of the Pantheon fascinates you most: its architecture, its engineering, or its history?

transcription.text: Did you know that when the Pantheon was originally constructed by Agrippa in the 1st century BC, he created a marvel of engineering using Roman cement? He did. Now while the version you see today of the Pantheon stems from the 118th version of Emperor Hadrian, he did model everything he did on Agrippa's Pantheon. Built on pozzolanic cement, which is a volcanic ash that exists all around the outskirts of Rome. This is an incredibly durable type of cement that hardens with time and even hardens with contact with water and was used for bridges. And the Pantheon is built in three different layers of cement. The very base one is actually made of basalt, then we have the pozzolanic cement in the middle and we use pumice stone cement on the top, making the Pantheon the biggest, largest, unreinforced concrete dome to this very day. It gets thinner and thinner the higher up it goes and it also gets lighter and lighter the higher up it goes. To add further to the ingenious construction, there's a wide open hole in the middle of the ceiling, which is about nine meters, 27 feet in diameter, bouncing the pressure back down to the ground. And there's also a copper ceiling. By cutting out a square in the ceiling, you take out the pressure four times and there's multiple squares within one. This isn't enough, the Pantheon is also built on a loose foundation, meaning when and if there's an earthquake, the entire building moves. The hole in the ceiling, just as a bonus, also adds to draining out the rainwater when it rains into the Pantheon. If you want to know any more great history about Rome or anywhere else, follow me, I'll lead the way.
